title: Geneva — Sales Pipeline
problem: Hunter Douglas India runs two sales divisions through disconnected tools. Pipeline visibility was manual. Leadership reporting required consolidating inputs from multiple people.
built: Unified sales pipeline platform — FastAPI + React + Supabase + Railway. Telegram bot for field reps. Role-based dashboards for reps, managers, leadership.
outcome: Time from field update to leadership visibility: days → hours
excerpt: Disconnected tools meant leadership visibility required manual consolidation across divisions. Built a unified pipeline platform with role-based dashboards and a Telegram bot for field reps.
context: Hunter Douglas India — 120 people across operations, manufacturing, and sales — operates two distinct sales divisions — Architectural Products (ceilings, facades, louvers) and Window Covering Products (blinds, curtains). Both divisions shared the same sales team structure but had no unified view of pipeline activity. At any given time, leadership had to wait for weekly Excel updates consolidated across 4–5 people before having a picture of where revenue was coming from. Field reps were on the road. They were not opening dashboards.
challenge: The core problem was not data — it was latency. By the time a rep updated a spreadsheet, a manager reviewed it, and leadership consolidated the picture, the information was 3–5 days old. Decisions were being made on stale context. Stage transitions were not being logged, so there was no way to identify where deals were dying.
approach: Map the existing workflow before touching any code — understand how reps actually update deals, not how the process was supposed to work
Design around the Telegram-first interface — reps are on their phones, not laptops. If it is not a message, they will not use it
Build role-based views from day one — rep, manager, and leadership have different information needs. One dashboard for everyone serves no one
Log every stage transition with timestamp and actor — the audit trail is the product
Deploy on Railway for simplicity — sales-sized team, not a startup that needs Kubernetes

decisions.decision: Telegram bot as the primary rep interface
decisions.rationale: Field reps spend the day on their phones. A web dashboard requires intent — opening a browser, logging in, navigating. A Telegram message is zero friction. Meets people where they already are.

decisions.decision: FastAPI over Django or Node
decisions.rationale: Async-first, fast to prototype, excellent Supabase integration. The team does not need the batteries Django includes — a clean REST API is all we need.

decisions.decision: Supabase over a self-managed Postgres instance
decisions.rationale: Built-in auth, realtime subscriptions, and row-level security. For a growing company, managed infrastructure is worth the cost.

decisions.decision: No mobile app
decisions.rationale: Building and maintaining a native app for the sales team is disproportionate effort. Telegram handles the mobile interface. Web handles the dashboard.

learnings: The best interface is the one people already use. Do not build a new habit — integrate with an existing one.
Stage velocity (how fast deals move through funnel) is more actionable than total pipeline value. We added velocity tracking in sprint 3.
Role-based access is not just a security feature — it is a UX feature. Showing a rep their colleague's pipeline adds noise, not insight.
Logging every action creates an audit trail that becomes invaluable for coaching conversations.

title: HD Hire — AI Applicant Tracking
problem: Hiring at a 120-person company is high-stakes and under-resourced. CVs pile up. Feedback gets lost. Good candidates go cold while the team is busy running the business.
built: AI-augmented ATS — Claude scores applications, ElevenLabs handles voice screening, candidates self-schedule via Cal.com. Full email automation via Resend.
outcome: Consistent, repeatable hiring process that does not depend on someone having bandwidth
excerpt: At a 120-person company, hiring is high-stakes and under-resourced. Built an AI-augmented ATS where Claude scores applications and ElevenLabs runs voice screening.
context: Every hire at a 120-person company has outsized impact. A bad hire in a small team does not get absorbed — it creates friction that affects everyone. And yet, the hiring process was the least systematised part of the operation. CVs arrived via email, were discussed over WhatsApp, and feedback was stored in someone's head. Candidates would go a week without hearing anything — not because no one cared, but because the team was busy running the business.
challenge: The constraint was not budget or intent — it was bandwidth. Screening CVs takes time. Scheduling calls takes coordination. Following up requires remembering. A small team simply does not have the overhead to run a rigorous process manually.
approach: Design the candidate experience first — a slow, disorganised process reflects on the company even when no one is hired
Use AI for the repeatable parts: CV scoring, initial screening, email follow-ups. Keep humans for the judgment calls
Self-scheduling eliminates the coordination loop — candidates pick a slot, no back-and-forth email chains
Voice screening via ElevenLabs handles the first conversation — evaluates communication, availability, and basics before a human is involved
Automate the full email lifecycle — every candidate gets a response

decisions.decision: Claude for CV scoring with structured JSON output
decisions.rationale: LLMs are remarkably good at reading CVs against a rubric and returning structured scores. The output is consistent, explainable, and auditable — better than gut feel and faster than manual review.

decisions.decision: ElevenLabs Conversational AI for voice screening
decisions.rationale: A phone screen handles communication quality, role fit basics, and availability check. ElevenLabs handles this at scale, any time of day, without coordinator bandwidth.

decisions.decision: Cal.com for self-scheduling
decisions.rationale: Eliminating the scheduling loop saves 2–3 email exchanges per candidate. At 50 applicants per role, that is significant overhead removed.

decisions.decision: Resend for email automation with React Email templates
decisions.rationale: Every candidate deserves a response. Resend makes transactional email reliable and templated — no one falls through the cracks.

learnings: AI scoring is most useful as a filter, not a decision. It surfaces the clear rejections quickly, freeing human attention for the interesting candidates.
Candidates self-schedule faster than expected — removing the back-and-forth meaningfully speeds the process.
PDF parsing is a genuine constraint — scanned CVs are not text-layer readable. Accepted as a known limitation in v1.
The hiring team dashboard needs to be opinionated about what is actionable today, not just a list of candidates.

screenshots.caption: HD Hire — AI Applicant Tracking — alt view

title: SpiceScience — RAG Knowledge App
problem: A book on spice flavour chemistry contains expert knowledge inaccessible to most cooks. The knowledge exists. The access does not.
built: Embedded 1,200+ knowledge chunks into Pinecone. Flask API + vanilla JS frontend. Spice Explorer, Custom Blend Creator, Cuisine recommendations.
outcome: Domain expertise from a specialist book, accessible via natural language
excerpt: Embedded 1,200+ knowledge chunks from The Science of Spice into Pinecone. Built a RAG system with Spice Explorer, Custom Blend Creator, and cuisine recommendations.
context: The Science of Spice by Stuart Farrimond is a rigorous book on the molecular chemistry of flavour — why certain spice combinations work, what compounds drive heat or sweetness, how aromatic profiles interact. Dense, scientific, and largely inaccessible to anyone who has not read and retained the whole thing. The insight: this book is a domain expert compressed into 300 pages. RAG could make that expert available on demand.
challenge: The experiment was about the pattern, not the product. Could you take a single book of dense domain knowledge and make it genuinely useful via natural language? If yes, the same approach applies everywhere — internal playbooks, compliance documents, technical manuals, coaching frameworks.
approach: Chunk the book into semantically coherent sections — not arbitrary character limits
Embed using Pinecone Inference API with multilingual-e5-large for high-quality semantic search
Build three distinct interfaces: Explorer (browse by spice), Blend Creator (describe a dish, get a recipe), Cuisine Guide (15+ world cuisines)
Keep the frontend deliberately simple — vanilla JS. The intelligence is in the retrieval layer, not the UI

decisions.decision: Pinecone Inference API for embeddings
decisions.rationale: Eliminates the need to manage a separate embedding model. Pinecone handles embedding and retrieval in one service — simpler architecture.

decisions.decision: 1,225 chunks from the full book
decisions.rationale: Chunk size tuned to preserve semantic context. Too small and you lose meaning, too large and retrieval precision drops. Tested at multiple sizes.

decisions.decision: Flask over FastAPI
decisions.rationale: Simplicity. The API surface is small, there is no async requirement, and Flask gets out of the way quickly for a proof of concept.

decisions.decision: No authentication, fully public
decisions.rationale: This was a proof of concept for the RAG pattern. Friction would kill the learning. Open access was the right call for an experiment.

learnings: RAG is most powerful on dense, structured knowledge that experts have but is not easily searchable. Books, manuals, frameworks — not general web content.
Chunk quality matters more than chunk quantity. 1,200 well-structured chunks outperformed 5,000 arbitrary splits.
The real insight was identifying the pattern: every organisation has the equivalent of this book. Expertise trapped in documents.
User questions reveal what the knowledge base cannot answer — a useful signal for identifying gaps.

title: CoachOS — Leadership Coach AI System
problem: A leadership coach with 30 years of client experience: the knowledge exists. The application is inconsistent because no system connects past sessions, frameworks, and client history at the moment it is needed.
built: AI system on Airtable + Obsidian + Pinecone + Claude. Surfaces institutional knowledge on demand, drafts session prep and follow-ups. Nothing reaches clients without coach approval.
outcome: Expertise available at scale; coach admin time reinvested into coaching quality
excerpt: 30 years of coaching expertise buried in notes and sessions. Built an AI system that surfaces institutional knowledge on demand and drafts session prep — nothing reaches clients without approval.
context: The coach had been working with leaders across industries for 30 years. That experience was real and valuable — but it was stored across handwritten notes, Word documents, Zoom recordings, and memory. Every new client engagement started from scratch. The coach could not easily pull up what had worked for a similar client two years ago, or which framework had unlocked a particular kind of stuck leader.
challenge: The design constraint was explicit: AI drafts everything, the coach approves everything. Nothing reaches a client without a human decision. This is not about replacing the coach — it is about giving the coach more time to exercise judgment.
approach: Use Airtable as the CRM — database-first, reliable, relational. The coach could already use it
Obsidian as the knowledge base — 30 years of frameworks in markdown, searchable and portable
Dual-layer Pinecone: one namespace for Obsidian (frameworks, methodologies), one for client documents (session notes, assessments)
Fireflies.ai for automatic meeting transcription — no manual export, no coach overhead
Make.com for automation orchestration — the coach is non-technical, no-code was the right choice
Claude for all drafting — session prep, follow-up summaries, client communications

decisions.decision: Dual Pinecone namespace for knowledge vs client data
decisions.rationale: Frameworks and client history are different retrieval contexts. Keeping them separate allows targeted retrieval.

decisions.decision: Fireflies.ai over manual transcription
decisions.rationale: Auto-joins meetings, transcribes, webhooks to the system. The coach conducts the session — the system handles the documentation.

decisions.decision: Make.com over custom code for automation
decisions.rationale: The coach needs to maintain and occasionally modify the automation. No-code means she can do it without a developer.

decisions.decision: Hard approval gate before any client communication
decisions.rationale: Non-negotiable design principle. AI outputs in coaching can cause real harm if they misread a situation. The coach's judgment is the product.

learnings: The hardest part was not the technology — it was building trust that AI would extend capacity, not replace judgment.
Dual-namespace RAG is significantly more precise than a single mixed index.
Session prep is the highest-leverage automation: 45-minute manual research reduced to a reviewed draft in minutes.
The same pattern applies to any expert who has accumulated knowledge over time — consultants, lawyers, doctors, senior operators.

title: Personal AI OS — 7-Agent Infrastructure
problem: Running a 120-person business while shipping software means context-switching constantly across operations, engineering, design, marketing, research, and health. Information gets lost between sessions. The same questions get answered again. And no single AI assistant can be a specialist at everything.
built: 7-agent AI team running on a Mac Mini: BarleyBot (CEO / orchestrator), Coffee (research), Coder (engineering), Picasso (design), TodoBot (chief of staff), MarketerBot (marketing), HealthBot (personal coach). Each bound to its own Telegram group. 4-layer memory architecture. 7 daily crons. Full constitution document and delegation protocol.
outcome: Active — compounds daily. Runs 24/7 on a Mac Mini.
excerpt: 7-agent AI team running 24/7 on a Mac Mini. BarleyBot orchestrates, Coffee researches, Coder builds, Picasso designs, TodoBot tracks, MarketerBot writes, HealthBot coaches. Each bound to a dedicated Telegram group. 4-layer memory + 7 daily crons. Governed by a constitution document.
context: At some point the question shifted from what should I build to what infrastructure do I need to build anything well. Each session with an AI assistant started from scratch — no memory of past decisions, no awareness of current projects, no context about what had already been tried.
challenge: Most AI assistant setups are stateless by default. Each conversation is isolated. The challenge was building persistent context without that becoming a maintenance burden in itself.
approach: Seven agents with distinct roles — CEO, research, engineering, design, chief of staff, marketing, coach — each bound to a dedicated Telegram group so context never crosses wires
BarleyBot as the single orchestrator — intakes ideas from Siva, delegates with full context, unblocks stuck agents, enforces the constitution, reports up
Four-layer memory: session context, daily logs, curated MEMORY.md, and a QMD semantic index rebuilt nightly
Seven daily crons: podcast digest, RSS digest, curated daily digest, QMD reindex, HealthBot morning briefing, Ideas of the Day, Daily Wrap + LinkedIn drafts
Constitution document defining review gates, delegation protocol, conflict resolution, and quality standards across the agent team
Each agent runs as a separate OpenClaw session with its own workspace and its own bot token

decisions.decision: Three specialist agents over one general agent
decisions.rationale: A general agent tries to do everything and does nothing well. Specialist agents with clear roles perform better and are easier to reason about.

decisions.decision: Telegram as the primary interface
decisions.rationale: Always-on, mobile-native, supports voice notes, file attachments, group chats.

decisions.decision: 3-layer memory over a single context file
decisions.rationale: Active context is ephemeral. Daily logs are raw. Long-term memory is curated. Each layer serves a different purpose.

decisions.decision: Groq Whisper for voice transcription
decisions.rationale: Fastest inference available for Whisper. Voice note to text in under 2 seconds.

learnings: Agents need dedicated Telegram groups. Running multiple agents through one DM creates context collisions and makes it impossible to ship in parallel.
BarleyBot delegates but never codes or researches directly. The moment the orchestrator starts doing specialist work, everything slows down.
Session files can bloat to thousands of lines and cause timeouts. Periodic truncation and fresh sessions are mandatory.
Claude Opus 4.6 is the right default for orchestration. GPT-5.4 with thinking: high is the right default for writing and coaching agents. Model choice matters.
A constitution document and explicit mission definitions per agent make delegation deterministic. Without it, agents drift.
Hot-reload config works for most settings but new sessions pick up thinking and fast mode cleanly — in-flight runs may need manual /think directives to update.

title: Command Center — Multi-Agent Orchestration Platform
problem: Running a 7-agent AI team via chat messages works until pipelines get long. Multi-step work — research then build then review then ship then promote — needs coordination, review gates, cost tracking, and a way to see what is running across agents without scrolling through Telegram.
built: A Next.js orchestration platform running as a LaunchAgent on the Mac Mini. Six blueprint pipelines (full-feature, research-build, bug-fix, marketing-campaign, design-review, new-project). SSE streaming for live run logs. Review gates routed to Telegram. Cost intelligence per agent. Projects + OKR framework. Files page with Obsidian links. Guardrails and context chain between phases.
outcome: Live — 115+ commits in March, v4 shipped in 6 sprints. Tailscale-accessible from mobile. Drives every multi-phase build across the agent team.
excerpt: A web orchestration platform running on the Mac Mini. Six blueprints (full-feature, research-build, bug-fix, marketing-campaign, design-review, new-project). Review gates routed to Telegram. Cost intelligence, Projects + OKRs, files, guardrails. 115+ commits in March to v4.
context: By late March the 7-agent team had proven it could run real work — sales pipeline builds, field apps, PWAs, a full DTC ops system. But coordinating multi-phase pipelines across agents was still manual: BarleyBot would delegate to Coffee for research, then Coder for build, then Picasso for UI review, then MarketerBot for promotion. Each handoff lived in Telegram messages. There was no single place to see what was running, what was waiting for approval, or what had cost how much.
challenge: The challenge was building a control surface without recreating the orchestrator logic that BarleyBot already handled. Command Center had to drive runs, expose review gates cleanly, and get out of the way. It also had to survive restarts, run as a background service, and be reachable from anywhere via Tailscale — not a local-only dev tool.
approach: Define blueprint pipelines as first-class objects — each blueprint declares phases, required agents, review gates, and expected outputs
Use Server-Sent Events for live run logs so the UI never polls and feels instantaneous
Route review gates through Telegram — the user approves, revises, or kills from the same chat interface where BarleyBot lives
Build a Files page with categories, markdown preview, and Obsidian deeplinks so outputs are browsable without leaving the UI
Track cost per agent per run with a Projects + OKR framework to tie runs back to goals
Ship as a LaunchAgent so it survives sleep and restarts — Tailscale handles remote access

decisions.decision: SSE over WebSockets for live logs
decisions.rationale: SSE is simpler, works through Tailscale and LaunchAgent without extra plumbing, and survives reconnection gracefully.

decisions.decision: Review gates in Telegram, not in the web UI
decisions.rationale: Siva is already in Telegram all day. Routing review gates through the web UI would add friction and context-switching for no benefit.

decisions.decision: LaunchAgent instead of Docker
decisions.rationale: The Mac Mini is the host. LaunchAgent is native, reliable, and zero-ops. Docker would add complexity without value for a single-user platform.

decisions.decision: Tailscale for remote access, not Cloudflare Tunnel
decisions.rationale: Tailscale is already running for other services and provides the trust boundary Siva needs — no public exposure.

learnings: Context preamble had to be rebuilt fresh on every restart — stale preamble was killing all build phases.
Piping prompts via stdin or file beats shell args — hit shell argument length limits early, now we stream big prompts in.
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY leaks from the OpenClaw gateway into spawned Claude Code processes broke every build phase until the fix landed.
Blueprint definitions should be short and essential — governance-v4 had too much prose and slowed down every phase until we truncated it.
Telegram review gates need three separate messages: header, output attachment, action prompt. One message tried to do everything and none of it worked.
A run that can not be killed from the same interface that started it is a run that will get abandoned.

title: Hillary's Ops OS
problem: Hillary's is a DTC window coverings brand under Hunter Douglas operating in Bangalore. 600+ homes served lifetime, 100+ active. The entire business ran manually — order intake, procurement, tailoring, installation, payment — each step in a different tool, no single system of record.
built: End-to-end order lifecycle OS. 6 role-based dashboards (CX, Procurement, Warehouse, Installation, Finance, Admin). 6 automated cascades that move orders through the pipeline without human intervention. Real-time action queues, rework routing, audit log.
outcome: A live business — 600+ homes, 100+ active orders — running on a single system built in 12 sprints
excerpt: Hillary's DTC window coverings business ran 600+ home installations manually. Built the full operational OS — 6 role dashboards, 6 automated cascades, real-time queues — in 12 sprints. Now the system of record for the whole business.
context: Hillary's is a direct-to-consumer window coverings brand operating under Hunter Douglas in Bangalore. Unlike B2B, DTC means every order is a distinct customer journey — intake, measurement visit, procurement, tailoring (for curtains), installation, payment. At 600+ homes served and 100+ active orders, managing this manually meant things fell through the cracks constantly. No one had a complete picture of where any given order was. Installers were coordinated over WhatsApp. Payment follow-up was reactive. Through March 2026, continued sprint development added an advance payment gate system (50% threshold with tranche tracking), a CX Partial Order Tracker with rework pipeline visibility, internal vs external installer classification, and Warehouse 'Awaiting Delivery' bucketing. The system is actively used daily by the full Hillarys India team.
challenge: The order journey for window coverings is genuinely complex. Blinds skip tailoring. Curtains need fabric procurement, then tailoring, then QC, then dispatch. Reworks can re-enter at multiple points. An order might have 4 POs, 3 GRN entries, a tailor assignment, a rework, and two installation visits — all of which need to stay in sync. The cascade logic alone took 4 sprints to get right.
approach: Map every role's workflow before writing code — CX, Procurement, Warehouse, Tailor, Installer, Finance each have distinct needs
Design the data model around the order lifecycle, not the org chart — one order record carries context through every stage
Build cascades for the handoffs that always happen the same way — when all GRNs are complete, auto-create the next stage record
Action-bucket UI for every role — not a list of orders, but 'here is what needs your attention right now'
Rework as a first-class flow — not an edge case. It happens enough to need its own routing logic
Supabase Realtime for live queue counts — no one refreshes a page manually in operations

decisions.decision: 6 role-based dashboards over a single admin view
decisions.rationale: A CX team member and a warehouse manager have completely different information needs. One dashboard forces both to filter noise. Six dashboards show each role exactly what they need to act on.

decisions.decision: Cascade architecture over manual handoffs
decisions.rationale: The 6 cascades (auto-create GRN when PO delivery is logged, auto-create installation when GRN is complete, etc.) eliminate the coordination overhead between roles. The system moves the order — people just do their step.

decisions.decision: Hard delete with full audit snapshot
decisions.rationale: Operations data needs to be recoverable. Every delete writes a JSONB snapshot of all related records to an audit log before cascading. Recovery is possible. Accidents are not catastrophic.

decisions.decision: Playwright E2E tests against the live deployment
decisions.rationale: Unit tests catch logic errors. But the real risk in an ops system is that role A's action breaks role B's queue. E2E tests against live Supabase catch the integration failures that matter.

learnings: Cascade logic is the hardest part of any multi-stage ops system. The edge cases (rework purchases re-entering the GRN flow, partial installations needing re-scheduling) require explicit design, not just handling.
Action-bucket UI is significantly more useful than a status list. 'Show me orders where I need to do something' beats 'show me all orders sorted by status'.
Supabase RLS + role-based routing is a clean pattern for this kind of system. Each role's dashboard query is already scoped — no accidental data exposure.
12 sprints of 15 tasks each is a surprisingly effective cadence for a system this complex. Small, shippable increments that each add a real workflow.

title: WalletBot — Family Finance App
problem: Two-income household, 13 expense categories, savings goals, no shared system. Tracking happened in WhatsApp messages and memory. Month-end was always a reconstruction exercise.
built: Telegram bot + Next.js dashboard. Natural language expense logging ('500 groceries', 'Zomato 850'). Claude classifies to 13 buckets. Salary tracking, savings goals, monthly P&L. Recharts dashboard with trends.
outcome: Family finance tracked in real time — no spreadsheets, no end-of-month guesswork
excerpt: Two-income household with no shared finance system. Built a Telegram bot where natural language expense logging ('Zomato 850') is classified by Claude into 13 buckets. Next.js dashboard for trends and goals.
context: Managing family finances with two incomes, a child, and 13 distinct spending categories is genuinely complex. The existing approach: WhatsApp messages, memory, and a mental model that got reconstructed every month-end. Every recurring question — how much did we spend on kids this month? are we on track for the Japan trip savings? — required digging through transaction histories manually.
challenge: Finance apps exist. The problem is adoption — they require behavior change, manual entry through an app, categorisation discipline. Most households start strong and abandon in week 3. The constraint was building something that fits into existing behavior, not trying to replace it.
approach: Telegram as the interface — both users are already in Telegram daily. Zero new app, zero new habit
Natural language entry — 'spent 1200 on Swiggy' or just '850 groceries'. No forms, no dropdowns
Claude classifies every expense into 13 buckets automatically — no manual categorisation
Shared state — both users can log, both see the same month's picture
Goals with allocation — 'save [goal] [amount]' sets aside money toward specific targets (Japan trip, emergency fund)
Next.js dashboard for the visual layer — monthly trends, bucket breakdown, goal progress via Recharts

decisions.decision: Telegram bot over a mobile app
decisions.rationale: A native app requires download, login, and forming a new habit. Telegram is already open. Meets both users exactly where they are, multiple times a day.

decisions.decision: Claude for expense classification
decisions.rationale: Rule-based classification breaks on 'Zomato', 'BigBasket', 'that parking thing'. Claude understands context and merchant names. Accuracy is significantly higher and requires no category maintenance.

decisions.decision: Monorepo (bot + web + db + shared)
decisions.rationale: Bot and dashboard share types, database queries, and utilities. A monorepo keeps this in sync without duplication. pnpm workspaces handle it cleanly.

decisions.decision: Supabase for shared state
decisions.rationale: Two users, one database. Supabase gives realtime, auth, and row-level security. Both users see the same month in real time.

title: SiteCapture — Field Check Measure App
problem: Hillarys India runs 3 field visits per customer order — sales, check measure, installation. No structured system connected them. Installers used paper notes and WhatsApp photos. CX manually bridged data between systems. Miscommunication caused rework.
built: iPad-optimized offline PWA for field and office teams. 4 role-based flows (CX, Installation Coordinator, Installer, Admin). Window-by-window photo capture, DC measurements, customer signature. Quote PDF parsing. Shared Supabase DB with Ops OS. 9 sprints, 69 commits in 4 days.
outcome: Structured site data — photos, measurements, customer sign-off — captured at check measure and carried through to installation
excerpt: Hillarys India's 3-visit customer journey ran on paper notes and WhatsApp. Built a field PWA for check measure and installation — photo capture, customer signature, quote PDF parsing — shared database with Ops OS. 9 sprints, 69 commits in 4 days.
context: SiteCapture is the third system in the Hillarys India stack, alongside Ops OS (order lifecycle) and InSite (sales/design consultation). Every order that needs a check measure goes through SiteCapture — CX creates the project, an installer visits site with an iPad to capture measurements and photos, CX reviews and generates a PDF, the customer signs off, and then installation is scheduled. Without SiteCapture, all of this happened via WhatsApp messages, paper forms, and manual data re-entry. The gap between what was measured on site and what the installer arrived with was the primary cause of rework.
challenge: Field tools have hard constraints: unreliable connectivity, multiple device sizes, roles with completely different tasks. An installer on site cannot be waiting for the server. CX in the office needs to see everything the installer captured, exactly as captured. The customer confirmation step — getting a digital signature and sharing a specification PDF — had to work as smoothly on an iPad in a customer's home as on a desktop in the office. Building offline-first without compromising data integrity is the hardest part.
approach: Offline-first from day one — all installer actions auto-save to IndexedDB, queue photos for upload when connectivity returns
Shared Supabase project with Ops OS — same DB, same auth, same roles. SiteCapture links to orders by JID. No separate auth system, no data duplication
Window-by-window capture model — each window is a discrete record with its own photos, measurements, and product confirmation. Installers work through windows one at a time
PDF generation on the server — quote PDF parsed from InSite output (pdfjs-dist), overlaid with SiteCapture data and customer confirmation
Customer signature at check measure completion — captured in-app, attached to the PDF, sent to customer before installation is scheduled
Hillarys brand guidelines applied across all surfaces — same design system as Ops OS for consistent team experience

decisions.decision: Shared Supabase project with Ops OS
decisions.rationale: The same database means financials (invoice amount, advance) flow automatically from the Ops OS orders table by JID. No sync, no API, no duplication. CX can see the full picture without switching systems.

decisions.decision: IndexedDB via Zustand persist for offline-first
decisions.rationale: Installers work on site with variable connectivity. Auto-save to IndexedDB ensures no data loss. Photo queue uploads when connectivity returns. The app never blocks on the server.

decisions.decision: 4 distinct roles, not one admin view
decisions.rationale: CX creates and reviews. Coordinators schedule. Installers capture. Admins manage. Each role sees only what they need to act on. One view for all roles would be unusable in the field.

decisions.decision: pdfjs-dist for quote PDF parsing
decisions.rationale: InSite generates a quote PDF. SiteCapture needs to extract confirmed measurements and product specs from it without manual re-entry. PDF parsing was the only non-manual option.

learnings: Offline-first is not a feature — it is a constraint that shapes the entire data model. Design for it from day zero, not as a retrofit.
Shared database across systems eliminates the sync problem entirely. More complex to model but dramatically simpler in operation.
Field roles and office roles have completely different UX needs. Pixel-perfect on desktop is irrelevant if the installer cannot use it with one hand on an iPad in a customer's home.
PDF parsing from a third-party system is fragile — any template change breaks it. Built with explicit fallback to manual entry.

metrics.label: Manual data re-entry
metrics.delta: shared Supabase DB with Ops OS

screenshots.caption: SiteCapture — Field Check Measure App — alt view

title: Vayu — Breathwork Training App
problem: Breathwork is evidence-backed for nervous system regulation, but most people default to one technique without understanding which method maps to which outcome. No feedback loop. No measurement. No consistent practice.
built: Personal PWA for structured breathwork. 12+ scientifically-grounded techniques classified by intent (Calm, Energize, Focus, Sleep, Recovery). Animated breath guide with Canvas-rendered organic form. Session streaks, insights dashboard, mood delta tracking. Origami design system — washi paper aesthetic, fold shadows, breath-speed animations. 10 sprints to v2.0.0.
outcome: Intent-matched technique selection with streak tracking and measurable mood improvement across sessions
excerpt: Personal breathwork PWA with 12+ techniques classified by intent. Canvas-rendered organic breath animation. Mood delta tracking across sessions. Origami design system — washi paper aesthetic, fold shadows, everything breathes. 10 sprints, v2.0.0 shipped.
context: Vayu started as a personal tool — a precision instrument for daily breath training, not a generic wellness app. The hypothesis: if you know which breathing technique to use for which physiological state (4-7-8 for sleep, Wim Hof for resilience, Box Breathing for focus), and you track the mood delta before and after each session, the data compounds into a picture of what actually works for you. Most apps give you guided audio. Vayu gives you a practice with measurement.
challenge: The design constraint was the hardest part: the UI had to reinforce the product's purpose. A breathwork app with snappy spring animations and hard geometric UI works against itself. Every animation curve, every shadow, every spacing decision is functional — it either adds calm or breaks it. The technical challenge was the Canvas-rendered breath animation — an organic luminous form that expands and contracts against a void, not a circle.
approach: 12+ techniques grouped by intent — Calm, Energize, Focus, Sleep, Recovery — so the selection decision is made by outcome, not by technique name recognition
Canvas API for the breath animation — organic, luminous, asymmetric form. Not a circle expanding. A shape that breathes
Mood delta as the core insight metric — pre/post session mood on a 5-point scale. The delta over time is the proof the practice is working

Origami design system — Japanese washi paper aesthetic. Fold shadows replace all borders. Instrument Serif for headings. Breath-speed easing on every transition (cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) — natural exhale deceleration)
Offline-first PWA — sessions work without connectivity. IndexedDB via Dexie.js for all local state
10 sprints from scaffold to v2.0.0, then post-ship origami UI redesign across all 4 screens

decisions.decision: Canvas API over SVG for the breath animation
decisions.rationale: The breath form is organic and asymmetric — not a path you can define in SVG. Canvas gives 60fps control over every pixel. The luminous glow, grain texture, and radial gradient required it.

decisions.decision: Intent-first technique classification
decisions.rationale: Most people know technique names but not when to use them. Grouping by outcome (Calm / Energize / Focus) makes the selection decision contextual. You pick by what you need, not what you remember.

decisions.decision: Offline-first with Dexie.js / IndexedDB
decisions.rationale: A breathwork session should never fail because of connectivity. All session data, technique library, and progress state live locally first.

decisions.decision: Origami design system post-ship
decisions.rationale: v2.0.0 was functional. The origami redesign was about integrity — a breathwork app should feel like it belongs to the practice. Paper, shadow, breath. Everything else would have been a mismatch.

learnings: Design language is product language. In a breathwork app, the UI either supports the state you're trying to induce or it undermines it. There is no neutral.
Intent-first classification is more useful than technique-first. Users know they're anxious — they don't know they want 4-7-8.
The Canvas breath animation is the product. Everything else — streaks, insights, library — exists to support consistent return to that screen.
10 sprints from zero to v2.0.0 with full offline support, audio engine, and insights dashboard is achievable with AI-assisted development and tight governance.
